headline: WHEAT JUMPS 87 CENTS AS FEEDERS SURGE
subheadline: Silver explodes 3% overnight while grains hold China's floor.
lead: Wheat gained 87 cents to $6.70¼, the strongest single-day move in three weeks, as funds rotated back into grains on renewed export optimism. Feeders surged 1.4% to $363.85, leading the livestock complex higher after yesterday's drift. The China $17 billion commitment is finding its legs two days later, but corn barely budged and beans stayed flat. This looks like sector rotation, not broad commodity revival.
the_takeaway: Wheat's leading the grain bounce, feeders confirming livestock strength.
teaser: Wheat surges as funds rotate back, feeders lead livestock higher.

one_number.context: Feeders led the livestock complex higher while live cattle stayed flat at $247.18, the first time in two weeks feeders outpaced live by more than a point. This divergence typically signals tight feeder supplies ahead of summer placement season, exactly what yesterday's cattle futures rally suggested was coming.

yesterdays_call.summary: Holding yesterday's China bounce but needs follow-through confirmation.
yesterdays_call.note: Wheat's 87-cent jump and feeder strength delivered the follow-through the grain complex needed.

sections.title: Wheat Leads Grain Rally
sections.body: Chicago wheat jumped 87 cents to $6.70, the biggest single-session gain since April's weather scare faded. **Funds are rotating back into grains**, but selectively. Wheat's export pace is running 8% ahead of last year while corn and soybeans stay range-bound. The China commitment is working through the complex unevenly, wheat first because the logistics are simpler than soybeans and the Russians are still offline. December corn gained a penny to $4.95 while July corn eased to $4.72. This is textbook fund repositioning: lead with the cleanest story, follow with the rest if it works.
sections.catalyst: USDA export sales data showing wheat pace 8% ahead of last year
sections.bottom_line: Wheat breaks out first, testing whether China deal has legs.
sections.vs_yesterday: Wheat: delivered the follow-through yesterday's bounce needed.

sections.title: Feeders Surge Past Live Cattle
sections.body: Feeder cattle surged 1.4% to $363.85 while live cattle held flat at $247.18, the first time in two weeks feeders outran live by more than a point. **Tight feeder supplies are showing up in the basis**, not just the futures. The feeder/live ratio touched 1.47, highest since March when placements were running behind seasonal pace. Friday's Cattle on Feed report will tell the placement story, but the futures are already pricing summer tightness. Live cattle volume was thin, waiting for the cash trade to reset. Beef cutouts held steady, no new catalyst there.
sections.catalyst: Tight feeder cattle supplies ahead of Friday's Cattle on Feed report
sections.bottom_line: Feeder strength confirms summer placement tightness showing up early.
sections.vs_yesterday: Feeders: broke yesterday's drift pattern, leading livestock higher.

sections.title: Silver Explodes Overnight
sections.body: Silver exploded 3% overnight to $76.03, the biggest single-session move since February's inflation scare. **Industrial demand is overwhelming investment selling** as electronics manufacturers rebuild inventory after the Hormuz disruption cut Asian supply chains. Gold gained a modest 0.7% to $4,498, confirming this is an industrial story, not monetary. Silver's 52-week range puts it at only 49% from the low while gold sits at 54%, room to run if the electronics cycle keeps accelerating. The dollar barely moved, so this isn't currency-driven.
sections.catalyst: Industrial silver demand from electronics manufacturers rebuilding inventory
sections.bottom_line: Silver leads metals higher on industrial demand, not monetary policy.
sections.farmer_action: Silver miners with exposure to industrial metals see margin expansion opportunity.

sections.title: Energy Holds Hormuz Floor
sections.body: WTI crude eased 1.7% to $101.88, giving back yesterday's Iran tension premium as two supertankers successfully exited the Strait of Hormuz bound for China. **The Hormuz floor is holding**, oil's not crashing despite successful transits. Natural gas stayed flat at $3.10, summer cooling demand not pricing yet. The India plan to send empty tankers directly to Gulf producers is the wild card, first time any major buyer has attempted direct loading since the blockade began. That succeeds, the risk premium comes off fast.
sections.catalyst: Two supertankers successfully exiting Strait of Hormuz with crude for China
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz risk premium intact despite successful tanker transits to China.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Revives Domestic Fertilizer Grant Program
outside_the_pit.body: Agriculture Secretary Rollins announced accelerated permitting and renewed grant funding to boost domestic fertilizer production, the first major policy push since the Russia supply shock began. The timing matters more than the dollars: spring application season is nearly over, but 2027 planning starts this fall.

outside_the_pit.title: Indonesia Tightens Export Controls on Key Commodities
outside_the_pit.body: The world's biggest nickel producer and top coal exporter is introducing new regulations to control commodity exports, President Subianto announced. Indonesia's moves typically signal broader emerging market resource nationalism when commodity prices run hot.

outside_the_pit.title: NASS Board Chair Warns on June Report Accuracy
outside_the_pit.body: The USDA's statistics board chair says farmer participation is critical for upcoming June Acreage and Quarterly Stocks reports, signaling potential data quality concerns. When NASS starts lobbying for responses publicly, the private estimates start mattering more.

spread_to_watch.label: Feeder cattle / live cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: The feeder premium is widening as summer placement season approaches and supplies tighten. When this ratio runs above 1.45, it typically signals feedlot buyers are paying up for cattle they can't find elsewhere. Friday's Cattle on Feed will confirm whether placements are running behind seasonal pace.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn firming on ethanol restart after maintenance
basis.body: **Corn basis east of the Mississippi is tightening** as ethanol plants restart after spring maintenance shutdowns. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with seasonal patterns and the Brazil harvest hitting export terminals. Soybean basis holding steady, waiting for old crop to clear before new crop logistics reset the board.

weekly_thread.question: Will China's $17 billion ag commitment shift fund positioning back into grains this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: Wheat's 87-cent surge suggests the funds are rotating back selectively, wheat first because export logistics are cleaner than soybeans. Corn and beans still waiting for their turn.

the_more_you_know.title: When feeders outrun live cattle by 1.4%, placement math is screaming
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 1.4% feeder surge while live cattle stayed flat isn't just a trading anomaly, it's placement economics in real time. When the feeder/live ratio hits 1.47, it means feedlot buyers are paying a 47% premium for 750-pound cattle they'll sell at 1,350 pounds in six months. That math only works when feedlots can't find cattle anywhere else, or when they're pricing in higher fed cattle prices this fall. Both scenarios point to tighter supplies ahead than the April Cattle on Feed report suggested.

watch_list.desc: Weekly export sales; wheat above 400K MT confirms rally has legs
Cattle on Feed report; placements below 95% of last year confirms feeder tightness
Silver above $77 breaks February highs, industrial demand story accelerating

daily_quote.text: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA Export Sales, Energy Information Administration, LSEG commodity data, Federal Reserve economic indicators
